speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel number nine of my interview with Robert Mendler. You were going to tell us about some special thoughts you had and some things we might have omitted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to really-- I'm very grateful to my uncle and aunt. They brought me over here to the United States, because it was his responsibility. I was the only one survivor in his family. I was very happy to be with him here. My aunt just passed way six months ago. My uncle died 30 years ago. I was very grateful to them. They really treat me like their own son. Not everybody was so lucky as I was. I came over here. My Uncle had a lot of pictures from my family, which brought a lot of good and bad memory to me. And I'm very grateful to be in these United States also. And I think whoever has a chance-- after all, like I said, the time is passing us, time is marching on. Have a chance from us to leave some legacy behind. Maybe the world will be a little bit more perfected with our help, I hope. I just hope nobody has to go through this what we went. Especially, I want to thank my wife also-- the patience, the months, the weeks, and the years that she put up with me. After all, she knew who I am. I'm not proud, because I'm different from somebody else, but I am. I can't help it. But for the patience, I want her-- thank you more than anybody else to be my wife for almost 43 years. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And there's no place like USA, believe me. I get up in the morning. I go to bed at night. If there is such a thing as God Almighty, I thank him for I'm here. I'm a free man. I can express myself. I can go and vote. I can-- I can do what I-- do what I feel like to do, as long as I don't harm somebody else. I'm a free man. That's the most important thing to me. I don't have to worry somebody's going to break my windows at night or something like that or to call me different names. Maybe they do sometime, but I can defend myself. For really, I'm very grateful to be here. And I want to thank you and this gentleman also to put up with me, spend so much time in my home. It was a very pleasure to have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about if we bring out your wife and introduce her then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to introduce to you my wife, Joan Pretter Mendler, my wife of 43 years. I want to thank her in person for all the time she put up with me, all the good time, all the bad time. She was a great help. She's a-- she's a pillar of my-- me, really. She's the one, she gave me the guts to go out and speak to people, spend time. I haven't spoke about it. I haven't talk about myself for many, many years. And she gives me a lot of encouragement. And I love her dearly for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. They've mostly been good years. The only thing-- the only regret I have, I didn't know any of my in-laws. That was taken from me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. My children didn't know how to say, grandpa. Unfortunately, my children grow up-- didn't know grandfather-- the same thing my great grandchildren, and my grandchildren-- my children, and my grandchildren. They don't know what grandfather is, unfortunate. But I'm happy to be-- have a family like my wife. They all believe in me. They all respect me. And I'm very grateful for that, for the little thing like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been a very happy life.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1920, in April, of my beloved parents, before they married, before their wedding, when they were engaged, in Nowy Targ, in Poland. This is my mother and my dad, Hermina Mendler Reibeisen and Mundek Reibeisen. [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] This is a wedding invitation. My beloved parents, they were married April, 1921, in Nowy Targ. I came across that. My uncle had it here in United States. I treasure this a lot-- something to remember-- my parents' wedding invitation. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1935 in Krakow, Poland. This is my father's side family, all the brothers and sisters, and my great uncle, my great aunt also. At the right, to my right, is my mother, my father, and my sister, my grandfather, my father's sister, Hannah-- she was getting married-- my grandmother to the left with my cousin [NON-ENGLISH], and my Uncle Yankel. And the rest are the brothers and sisters-- all disappeared due to the Nazis. All went to the gas chambers-- burned in Belzec, 1942-- what a shame. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother's side family in Europe. This picture was taken 1930. My uncle, in the middle of the picture, standing behind my aunt, Emil Mendler, came for a visit from the United States in 1930. It's my mother's side family, what's left. At the left, to my left, I should say, my father, my brother, my sister, my mother. Next to my father is my cousin, Frydka, my uncle Emil from United States, my uncle, Dr. Jacob Mendler, my cousin, Anda Mendler, my uncle, Gustav Korn, my aunt-- mother's sister, Mala Korn, her daughter, Anna Korn, my grandfather, Heinrich Mendler, my aunt, Regina Mendler, and myself, right in the middle in front of my grandfather. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother. It was taken 1928 when she was a young lady. In the background, that's part of our country in the mountains. It's really a nice picture. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Beautiful picture. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself and my brother. I was two years old in this picture, and my brother, five years old. It was taken 1927 or '28. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] My brother name was Gienek. He was 17 years old when he came to Auschwitz, direct to the ovens, the gas chamber. I haven't seen him since 1942. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] This picture was taken of my sister, Henia, to the left; and my two cousins, Leo and Ania. My sister was ten years old when she went to gas chamber in Belzec. My cousin Leon, he was six years old when he went to the gas chamber in Belzec. My cousin, Ania, she was also 10 years old when she went to the gas chamber in Belzec. This picture was taken just before the war, 1937-- '38-- something like that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah. This picture was taken also in Nowy Targ-- my Aunt Mala with husband, Gustav, their daughter, Ania, and the son, Leon. Unfortunate, they all went to Belzec's gas chambers-- senseless-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --so young. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1938, just before the war, front of our house. To the left, that's me. Next to me is a friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH]. He's-- he's gone. [NON-ENGLISH], he lives in New York. He survived. In the middle is my brother, Gienek. He's gone. [NON-ENGLISH], he lives in his Israel. And [NON-ENGLISH] Turner-- he lives also in Israel. So three of us-- no, five of us-- four of us survive. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In front of our house, this picture was taken. [PAUSES FOR 36 SECONDS] This picture was taken also in Nowy Targ, in our organization, Akiva, which is like a boy scout, a scout-- girl and boys. In this picture, there's only three of us left from all the group alive today-- myself and two other friends of mine. They all went to the gas chambers or murdered some different fashion by the Nazis. [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] This picture was taken in the ghetto, in 1941. As you notice, some of friends of ours, we wear the white armband with the Star of David. The two end one, you can see that. From all in the picture, I'm the only one alive today. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the-- on the right side, on the bottom, it's Henek Feler, which-- which we adopt him at the market. If the parents get into fight, they left the children standing. So we adopt the little fella. He was like my brother. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We start from the bottom on the left side-- Monek Weiss. We call him [NON-ENGLISH]. Poldik Ledenberg, Henek Feler, standing up at the right, in the glasses; Romek Statter, Emil Kaufus. myself, and Benek Kalfus. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The picture was taken in the ghetto, 1941. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] See the wall against-- behind us from the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my uncle, Emil Mendler. He live in Latrobe with his wife, Mini Mendler. They had a sponsor bill for me being here. They brought me over here after I was liberated from the concentration camps, in Latrobe. Unfortunate, they're all gone also, already. I just-- my aunt just passed away about six months ago. My uncle died thirty years ago. And I'm very grateful to them, again, to brought me over to these United States of America-- Emil and Mini Mendler. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Unfortunate. Finally, after all these years bacheloring-- to be a bachelor-- I finally took a step forwards. I got married to Joan Pretter, and she's my wife today. We've been married 43 years-- very happy. She's a wonderful person, not because she's my wife. She's my partner. She understands me. She puts a lot with me. I love her dearly for that. She's a good human being-- Joan Pretter Mendler, mother of my children. [PAUSES FOR 38 SECONDS] This is memorial plaque in the first [NON-ENGLISH], in Jerusalem, on Mount Sinai. It's a plaque to memory of our loved ones from Nowy Targ and surrounding villages-- Nowy Targ, Zakopane, [NON-ENGLISH], and all the others. It's in Jerusalem on Mt. Sinai. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Brussels, Belgium. On the left, it's myself. In the middle is my friend, [NON-ENGLISH]. He lives in Antwerp. And on the right, Siegfried. He lives in Sweden-- Uppsala. As I mentioned before about my friend [NON-ENGLISH], that he was killed in Israel during the war for independence. It wasn't true, because 1980 or '81, when we went to Jerusalem for our first Holocaust conference reunion, I found somebody which knew him. Soon as I received his number from Belgium, I went to the hotel, and I call him right away. And I was very excited to find out he was alive. Year later, I pay him a visit, and here I am, my first visit. I didn't-- he didn't know where I was. I didn't know where he was. And we all thought he was killed for the war for independence in Israel. But thank God he's alive. And I keep in touch with him. This was my best buddy, my best friend. We spent many years together all through the camps. And we were liberated together too-- my best friend. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I like to tell you a bit story about my friend [NON-ENGLISH], in the middle. He lives in Antwerp, Belgium. Like I said before, we all thought he died in 1948 for the war of independence in Israel. Meantime, in 1981, when I was in Israel for the Holocaust reunion with a group from Pittsburgh, Mark Stone and others, we went to the kibbutz near [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: We were walking around. And a fellow come by, passed me by and stopped me, and slapped me in my face. He puts a hand on my shoulder, and he asks me, don't you know me? Don't you remember me? I was so shook up, I was speechless. I couldn't talk for about a five minutes. I thought I was taking a heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he started to talk to me in [NON-ENGLISH]. Finally, I came to myself, and I start to cry, and him too. This was one of us foursome when we escaped from the last camp in Pocking. We were hidden in a barn. He lives in Israel. His brother lives in New York. His name is Adam [NON-ENGLISH]-- Adam Naor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, it's too bad [NON-ENGLISH] is not with us today. Would-- he would be very happy. Too bad he's-- he was killed. So he said to me, no. He's alive! I said, how do you know? It was a shock to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, my daughter was in Belgium on a vacation, and he met his daughter at the pool. They were talking about each other, about their parents, and she mentioned it about [NON-ENGLISH]. And she said-- the other girl from Belgium-- [NON-ENGLISH] is my father. He's alive. There's only one [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's what he found out about him. They gave me his telephone number, and I went to the hotel at night. I called right away from Israel to Antwerp, placed a call. I woke him up midnight-- after midnight-- and I started to talk to him. I was so happy to hear from him. A short time later, I came back home to United States. I took a trip to Belgium. I was very happy to find him alive. From now on, we all always keep in touch with each other-- some happy occasion we had. That's [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a new generation of Mendlers. As you can see, myself and my wife, we're holding our grandchildren. In front of me is my-- my older grandson, David. He's eight years old today. There there's Max. He lives in Derry, here. He's 3 and 1/2. And our little Mickey, he's 1 year and 1/2. To the left is my youngest son, Mark, with his wife, Becky. In the middle is my oldest son, Michael, with his wife, Stacy. I'm very happy to have them so close here. Now they can carry on forward, I hope. I had to start from beginning. I'm the only one survivor of the whole family. And I'm happy to have two boys and three grandsons. They have to carry on, I hope. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] I'm happy to have Mark and Becky, and my two grandchildren close by me. We see each other as often as I can. I love my grandchildren-- of course, my kids also-- happy to have them so close. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1935, just before my grandfather passed away. They were visiting with my mom and dad. It's the back of our house. My-- to the left is my father's sister, Balcia, my grandfather, my mother, my dad, my sister in front of my father, and myself in front of my mom. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just a few months later, my grandfather took a heart attack. He passed away. At least, thank God, he died a natural death. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This is my Uncle Jacob-- Jacob Mendler, they called him. He was Dr. Jacob Mendler. He used to live in [NON-ENGLISH] before he was married. He died a tragic death-- terrible death. He lived through the war in hiding. Two days before the liberation of Poland, he was murdered. He was killed. He was betrayed by his own wife-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --in hiding. He was my godfather. I used to love him dearly. [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS] This was my first identification paper in Germany, which they call Ausweis. As you see, it tells you where I was born and some of the camps where I spent time. This picture was taken maybe, I think, about on the 5-- 4, 5 months after liberation. I still didn't had no hair, so I wear my hat from concentration camps. I got a shirt from army friend of mine-- American army soldier. And I got a suit from a German family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After my sickness, my neck was still swollen, full with water. I was 18 years-- 18 and 1/2 years old in this picture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1945. [PAUSES FOR 40 SECONDS] I wore this hat for a good while until my hair started to grow. [LAUGHS] I was ashamed to go out without a hat.
